title: Service-lane changes were corrected because they were left in docs and CLI instead of the dashboard surface
expected_state: After the service-lane work changed how the human would interact with the system, the dashboard should have been updated to reflect or offer that path, or the agent should at least have surfaced the choice explicitly in the human's form factor.
actual_state: The produced outcome still left the human to discover the new service-lane shape through docs and command-line usage instead of through the dashboard surface or an explicit offer, so the human-facing form factor lagged behind the real workflow change.
human_costs: the human had to notice that the dashboard surface had not been updated to match the new behavior
the human was pushed toward docs and command-line steps for a workflow that should have been surfaced for them

human_intervention.summary: The human rejected the docs-and-CLI handoff and restated that the dashboard form factor or an explicit offer should carry the workflow for them.

feedback_quotes: Why didn't you update my dashboard? Or at least offer it.
Humans don't want to read your docs and use the command-line
The only question really should be "what does the human prefer we do?"

entries.target_state: Workflow changes that matter to the human should be surfaced in the dashboard or offered there, not left implicit in docs and CLI steps.
The human-facing form factor should spare humans from having to read docs and use the command line just to act on the new workflow quickly.
The agent should either update the dashboard or explicitly offer the human-facing alternative instead of silently hoping the gap goes unnoticed.
The deciding question should be what the human would prefer the agent to do, not whether the human might miss the dropped ball.
